source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.1.2-8.1.4 fdisk/cfdisk/parted (pp. 128-130)

questions.q: List all partitions on all disks using fdisk.
questions.why: fdisk -l lists logical devices; listing is really the only reason to still reach for fdisk.

questions.q: What are the limits of fdisk?
options: It uses MBR partition tables only - no GPT, and cannot handle disks larger than 2 TB
It only works on GPT disks
It only works on removable media
questions.why: fdisk is MBR-only, so it cannot handle GPT or disks over 2 TB; its handy use is 'fdisk -l' to list.

questions.q: Between fdisk and cfdisk, which does the guide recommend for managing partitions, and why?
options: cfdisk - it is more reliable and better optimised
fdisk - it is the only interactive one
questions.why: Both are interactive, but cfdisk is more reliable and better optimised, so it is preferred.

questions.q: Open the interactive partition manager cfdisk on the disk /dev/sda.
questions.why: cfdisk <device> opens the interactive menu to manage that disk's partitions.

questions.q: What advantage does parted have over fdisk?
options: It partitions without fdisk's drawbacks and can even recover (rewrite) a deleted partition table
It only works from a GUI
It is limited to 2 TB like fdisk
questions.why: parted (partition editor) avoids fdisk's limits and includes a recovery function to rewrite a deleted partition table; gparted is its GUI.

questions.q: Without LVM, what are the five steps of preparing physical media, in order?
options: Set up the disk, partition, create file systems, mount them, manage user access
Mount, partition, format, set up the disk, manage access
Create file systems, set up the disk, partition, manage access, mount
Partition, mount, manage access, format, set up the disk
questions.why: The order is: set up the physical disk, partition, create the file systems, mount them into the tree, then manage user access.
